original_abstract:
The widespread adoption of multi-sensor technology and the proliferation of massive datasets have exposed the limitations of traditional "flat-view" matrix models, necessitating a shift toward more versatile analytical tools. This paper highlights how higher-order tensors—multiway arrays—facilitate a fundamental paradigm shift toward models that are essentially polynomial in nature. Crucially, unlike matrix-based methods, the uniqueness of these tensor decompositions is guaranteed under very natural and mild conditions. 

Leveraging the mathematical rigor of multilinear algebra, tensor techniques offer exceptional flexibility in applying constraints that align with specific data properties. This allows for the extraction of more nuanced latent components that matrix methods often fail to capture. We provide a comprehensive introduction to tensor decompositions from a signal processing perspective, tracing the journey from algebraic foundations and basic models—such as Canonical Polyadic (CPD) and Tucker—to advanced multi-view analysis and cause-effect schemes. Furthermore, we demonstrate how the tensor framework generalizes core paradigms including signal separation, subspace techniques, and classification. To address the "curse of dimensionality" associated with big data, we discuss computational strategies inspired by compressed sensing and scientific computing. Supported by real-world case studies, this work illustrates the power of tensors as efficient tools for modern signal processing, machine learning, and data analysis.
humanized_abstract:
Multisensor systems and large datasets have made the traditional matrix approaches (flat-view) obsolete. Higher-order tensors (multiway arrays) are used to build new polynomial-based models. It is shown that the tensor decompositions are unique under very mild conditions.

Unlike matrix based approaches, the mathematical structure of multilinear algebra inherent to tensor methods allow for more flexible application of assumptions on the data. This facilitates the extraction of much finer details of the underlying latent variables and connections; details that are often lost with the more standard matrix based approaches. This book provides a unified survey on tensor decompositions from a signal processing perspective. We start with the basic algebra and models including canonical polyadics (CP) and the Tucker decomposition and then proceed with more advanced multi-view and cause–effect analysis. We show that the tensor format also generalizes well-known signal processing concepts such as: blind source separation, subspace methods and classification, while at the same time addressing the inherent difficulties encountered with large dimensional data sets; a problem also arising in the field of big data, for which fast and efficient solutions are sought in the fields of compressed sensing and scientific computing. The book provides many useful examples and demonstrations of the validity of the tensor based signal processing approaches and tools with numerous applications to both traditional and modern fields of data analysis, machine learning and signal processing.
